title: Existence and uniqueness of Green's function to a nonlinear Yamabe problem
speaker: Yanyan Li
abstract: Abstract: For a given finite subset S of a compact Riemannian manifold (M; g) whose Schouten curvature tensor belongs to a given cone, we establish a necessary andsufficient condition for the existence and uniqueness of a conformal metric on $M \setminus S$ such that each point of S corresponds to an asymptotically flat end andthat the Schouten tensor of the new conformal metric belongs to the boundary of the given cone. This is a joint work with Luc Nguyen.
tags: Workshop on Geometric Functionals: Analysis and Applications

proof_stages.label: Nonlinear conformal Green-function formulation
proof_stages.summary: Starting from the conformal-Laplacian Green function, the speaker reformulates the problem using the Schouten tensor, admissible eigenvalue cones, conformal metrics, boundary-cone equations, and prescribed pole strengths.

proof_stages.label: Cone threshold parameter
proof_stages.summary: The Gårding-type cones gamma_k and the number mu_gamma are introduced to measure the singularity threshold, with mu_gamma_k=(n-k)/k separating the regimes k<n/2 and k>=n/2.

proof_stages.label: Existence/nonexistence and measure theorems
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states existence and uniqueness of a C^0 viscosity, in fact C^{1,1}, Green function with arbitrary finite poles and strengths when mu_gamma^+>1, nonexistence except for the round-sphere one-pole case when mu_gamma^+<=1, and a smooth-approximation theorem whose normalized sigma_k measures converge to Dirac masses at the poles.

proof_stages.label: Role in sigma_k Yamabe compactness
proof_stages.summary: The speaker motivates the theorem as a needed substitute for the Green-function and positive-mass inputs in Yamabe compactness theory, especially for possible blow-up analysis in the sigma_k Yamabe problem.

proof_stages.label: Nonexistence by Bishop-Gromov rigidity
proof_stages.summary: In the mu_gamma^+<=1 regime, the cone condition gives nonnegative Ricci curvature for the conformal metric; its asymptotically Euclidean end makes the Bishop-Gromov volume ratio equal to one at both small and large scales, and a continuous-metric viscosity version of volume comparison and rigidity forces the exceptional Euclidean/round-sphere geometry.

proof_stages.label: Existence via opened cones and degenerate estimates
proof_stages.summary: For the mu_gamma^+>1 regime, the proof solves perturbed sigma_k equations in slightly opened cones with carefully scaled boundary data and barriers, obtains C^0, gradient, Hessian, and closeness-to-radial estimates uniform enough to pass to the Green-function limit and to identify the limiting Dirac measure.

proof_stages.label: Viscosity comparison uniqueness
proof_stages.summary: The approximating functions converge to the constructed Green function while serving as strict supersolutions, and perturbable smooth subsolutions allow the standard viscosity comparison argument to give uniqueness.

examples.name: Linear conformal-Laplacian Green function and mass
examples.note: The standard Yamabe/conformal-Laplacian Green function with its pole expansion and positive-mass term is used as the template for the nonlinear Green function and mass questions.
examples.engagement_reason: He sets up the linear conformal-Laplacian Green function, writes its standard expansion and states the positive mass theorem, but does not derive or compute it.

passes.reason: It is used as the standard linear Green-function/positive-mass prototype for the nonlinear problem, not as a sporadic or equality-case object.

examples.name: Degenerate Monge-Ampere regularity comparison
examples.note: The determinant/Hessian equation and Lampert's degenerate Monge-Ampere Green functions illustrate why local higher regularity is not expected, while global smoothness might still occur.
examples.engagement_reason: He only uses degenerate Monge-Ampere as a regularity comparison, citing determinant-type degeneracy and Lampert’s smoothness result.

passes.reason: The det-Hess equals zero Monge–Ampère model is a degenerate boundary case cited to show that local higher regularity cannot generally be expected.
The comparison is to the genuinely degenerate Monge-Ampere equation such as det Hessian = 0, used as a regularity analogy.

examples.name: Classical Yamabe compactness blow-up template
examples.note: The k=1 Yamabe compactness story, where blow-up limits normalize to Green functions and positive mass rules out noncompactness, supplies the strategy the speaker wants to parallel for sigma_k.
examples.engagement_reason: He traces the Yamabe blow-up compactness template: finite blow-up points, normalization, convergence to a Green-function combination, and the role of positive mass.

passes.reason: A Yamabe blow-up sequence is the limiting noncompact enemy case for the desired compactness or a-priori estimate.
The Yamabe blow-up pattern is invoked as the noncompact enemy scenario for a priori compactness estimates.
the Yamabe blow-up configuration is a limiting loss-of-compactness case built from critical bubbles and is the enemy scenario in compactness arguments
the Yamabe blow-up/bubble template is the critical Sobolev-Yamabe extremal and represents the enemy case to compactness.
A Yamabe blow-up profile is the limiting boundary case for compactness and serves as the adversarial failure scenario for an a priori bound.

A Yamabe blow-up sequence is the enemy case for compactness and has a singular concentration/Green-function limit at the boundary of the compactness problem.
Yamabe blow-up is the adversarial noncompactness pattern, sitting at the critical limiting concentration regime

examples.name: Locally conformally flat sigma_k mass picture
examples.note: The known locally conformally flat case, with finitely many standard bubbles and a local expansion with nonnegative mass-like term and rigidity, illustrates the desired positive-mass counterpart in a special setting.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes the locally conformally flat sigma_k punctured-ball model and its asymptotic decomposition, then reasons through the nonnegative mass and rigidity consequences.

passes.reason: The locally conformally flat sigma-k mass model solves the boundary cone equation and is used as a positive-mass analogue rather than to refute a claim.
The locally conformally flat sigma_k mass theorem is a systematic special setting giving a positive-mass analogue, not a sporadic or adversarial example.
